relevance.reason: Higher-rate income or substantial wealth — significant tax planning opportunities

observations.text: Income of £120,000 puts you in the £100k–£125,140 Personal Allowance taper band. PA reduced to £2,570, costing £4,000 in additional tax. Effective marginal rate in this band is 60%.
SIPP £850,000: 25% tax-free cash entitlement is £212,500, well within your £268,275 Lump Sum Allowance.
You have £140,000 of pension contribution headroom this year (£60,000 current AA + £80,000 carry-forward). At your marginal rate of 40%, full use would generate up to £56,000 in tax relief.
Your GIA holds £12,000 of unrealised capital gains. Unused £3,000 CGT Annual Exempt Amount expires April 5 (319 days). Cannot carry forward.
SIPP of £850,000 enters Inheritance Tax estate from 6 April 2027 (320 days). At current value, this exposes £340,000 to 40% IHT on death post-2027 if estate exceeds nil-rate bands.
observations.citation: Finance Act 2026 s.[pension-iht-inclusion] (Royal Assent 18 March 2026, effective 6 April 2027)

recommendations.headline: Sacrifice £20,000 into pension to escape the £100k taper trap
recommendations.drill_down: Your income of £120,000 is in the £100k–£125,140 Personal Allowance taper band where your effective marginal rate is 60% (40% income tax + 20% from the lost £1-per-£2 PA reduction). Salary-sacrificing £20,000 into your pension would bring your taxable income to £100,000, restoring your full Personal Allowance and saving approximately £12,000 in tax this year. The sacrificed amount also accrues in your pension for retirement.
action_steps: Confirm employer offers salary sacrifice (most do)
Request £20,000 sacrifice — typically arranged for next payroll
Confirm employer NIC saving (13.8% / 15%) is rebated to you (some employers split this)

impact.time_horizon: this tax year + every future year you exceed £100k

risk.reversibility: irreversible once contributed — locked until age 55+

recommendations.citation: ITA 2007 s.35 (PA taper) + FA 2004 s.188 (pension contributions)
recommendations.flip_conditions: If you need the cash now for liquidity, ISA may be preferred even with lower tax efficiency
recommendations.fca_boundary: Information only — salary sacrifice has irreversible consequences. Speak to a qualified financial adviser before acting.
common_mistakes: Over-sacrificing past £60k AA → 45% Annual Allowance charge
Forgetting MPAA if you have already flexi-accessed any pension

recommendations.headline: Phase your £212,500 tax-free cash over 10 years instead of lump sum
recommendations.drill_down: Taking your full £212,500 25% tax-free cash in one year forces the remaining 75% (£637,500) into drawdown and likely additional-rate tax bands. Instead, crystallise £21,250 per year for 10 years. Each year you take £5,313 tax-free + £15,938 taxable. The taxable portion stays within basic-rate band where possible. Estimated tax saving vs lump-sum approach: ~£26,960 over the period, with the additional benefit that uncrystallised SIPP balance continues to grow tax-free.
action_steps: Open a flexible drawdown arrangement with your SIPP provider
Coordinate with state pension start (age 66/67) to avoid stacking
Review yearly — adjust if rules change or circumstances shift

risk.downside: death post-75 means beneficiaries pay marginal tax on remaining funds

assumptions.life_expectancy: assumes ≥10 years to take advantage of phasing
assumptions.tax_rules_stable: phased over 10 years — Budgets may change rules
assumptions.returns: assumes pension grows at sustainable rate

recommendations.flip_conditions: If life expectancy <5 years, lump-sum + spend may dominate. If estate already at IHT cap, accelerating drawdown may be preferred to push assets out via gifting.
recommendations.fca_boundary: Information only. Pension crystallisation is irreversible — speak to a qualified pension specialist before acting.
common_mistakes: Phasing across more years than life expectancy supports
Not factoring in state pension when it starts
Crystallising past £268,275 Lump Sum Allowance and triggering additional charges

recommendations.headline: Bed-and-ISA: move £12,000 from GIA to ISA before April 5
recommendations.drill_down: You hold £380,000 in your General Investment Account (GIA), exposed to income tax on dividends and CGT on gains. Your ISA has £12,000 unused allowance this year. Sell £12,000 from your GIA — using your £3,000 CGT Annual Exempt Amount to minimise CGT — and immediately re-buy the same holdings inside your ISA. Future dividends and gains on this portion are then tax-free permanently. ISA allowances cannot be carried forward, so missing this deadline loses the wrapping opportunity for these specific funds.
action_steps: Calculate which GIA holdings have lowest unrealised gains (minimise CGT)
Sell £12,000 worth of these GIA holdings
Immediately re-buy the same holdings inside your ISA (most platforms automate this as "Bed and ISA")

risk.reversibility: irreversible once sold — but can sell from ISA later if needed
risk.downside: one-day market exposure between sale and repurchase

recommendations.citation: ISAR 1998 Reg 4 (annual limit) + TCGA 1992 s.3 (CGT AEA)

assumptions.aea_unused: assumes £3,000 CGT AEA not already used this year

recommendations.flip_conditions: If GIA gains exceed £3,000 AEA, partial bed-and-ISA may be better than full sale.
recommendations.fca_boundary: Information only. Bed-and-ISA timing matters — consult your platform and adviser.
common_mistakes: Selling holdings with large gains and breaching CGT AEA
Forgetting to instruct re-purchase — leaving money in cash
Bed-and-ISA-ing a Lifetime ISA position (different rules)

recommendations.headline: Pre-April-2027 SIPP strategy: 320 days to plan
recommendations.drill_down: From 6 April 2027, defined contribution pensions (including SIPPs) enter the estate for Inheritance Tax. Your £850,000 SIPP currently sits outside the estate. From April 2027, this exposes up to £340,000 to 40% IHT on death (subject to nil-rate bands). Strategies to consider: (a) accelerate phased crystallisation pre-2027 to spend SIPP first; (b) gift surplus drawdown income to remove from estate (s.21 IHTA exemption for regular gifts out of normal expenditure); (c) review nominations to favour adult children with own pension allowance. The April 2027 effective date is fixed — Royal Assent received 18 March 2026.
action_steps: Decide: spend SIPP first (favours pre-2027) vs preserve SIPP for beneficiaries (favours pre-75 death)
Review beneficiary nominations for tax efficiency
Project IHT exposure under both scenarios with adviser
Re-check April 2027 — possible scheme to delay further (legislation still evolving)

risk.reversibility: crystallisation irreversible; gifting subject to 7-year clock
risk.downside: spending SIPP early loses tax-free growth

recommendations.citation: Finance Act 2026 (Royal Assent 18 March 2026) — pension IHT inclusion from 6 April 2027

assumptions.law_stable: assumes April 2027 implementation date holds (Government has confirmed)

recommendations.flip_conditions: If life expectancy >20 years, the time value of SIPP growth may exceed IHT cost.
recommendations.fca_boundary: Information only. SIPP and IHT planning is high-stakes — speak to a Chartered Tax Adviser AND pension specialist together.
common_mistakes: Treating April 2027 as the gift deadline (it is the IHT inclusion date — gifts still need 7-year clock)

recommendations.headline: Realise £2,500 of capital losses to offset future gains
recommendations.drill_down: You have £2,500 of unrealised losses in your GIA. Crystallising these before April 5 establishes them as offsettable losses — they can offset realised gains in the same year (after using your £3,000 AEA) and carry forward indefinitely. To avoid the 30-day "bed-and-breakfast" rule, replace the sold holding with a similar-but-not-identical fund (e.g. swap a Vanguard FTSE 100 tracker for an iShares FTSE 100 tracker).
action_steps: Identify GIA holdings with unrealised losses
Replace with a similar but not identical fund (different provider, same index)

risk.reversibility: partial — re-buying same security within 30 days disallows the loss

recommendations.flip_conditions: If you expect holdings to recover strongly within 30 days, holding may dominate.
recommendations.fca_boundary: Information only. Loss harvesting is subject to anti-avoidance rules — speak to a Tax Adviser.
common_mistakes: Bed-and-breakfasting via spouse — also caught by anti-avoidance
Not declaring the loss — required on Self Assessment

what_if_prompts: What if I sacrifice £20,000 of my bonus into pension this year?
What if I start phased pension crystallisation next year?
What if I delay state pension by 3 years?
What if I bed-and-ISA before April 5?
What if I move to Scotland — what would my income tax change to?
